title: Freehand: a page with data
checkLabels.t4: A header row and three more
checkLabels.t14: The table is inside the main
why.empty: Empty editor. Start from the table, not the page. It is the core — everything else exists for it.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order. In tables this is the most common mistake — a single goes missing easily.
hint2.not-closed: They close from the inside out: before , before .
why.no-table: There is no table. The task asks for a comparison — something with rows and columns. If you lined the data up with spaces, look at what comes out on the right: spaces line up nothing.
hint2.no-table: What in your topic gets compared? Prices. Years. Sizes. Ratings. Each of those has rows and columns.
why.two-tables: There is more than one table. The rule was: exactly one. A second one usually means you are laying something out with it — and that is the mistake from the last lesson.
hint2.two-tables: Ask about the second one: what is it? What does one of its cells mean? No answer — it is not a table, it is a grid.
why.too-small: The table is too small. It needs a header row and at least three rows of data under it. Two things are not a comparison — they are a pair.
hint2.too-small: Three rows of data means three things you are comparing. Three bikes, three recipes, three days.
why.no-th: The table has data but no header row. A first row that looks different is not a header row. It has to SAY so — otherwise the browser reads your column names as just another data cell.
hint2.no-th: Three columns means three names. All three cells in the first row are <th>, not just the first.
why.empty-th: There is an empty header cell. An empty name names nothing.
hint2.empty-th: Check the word stayed INSIDE the tag: <th>Price</th>, not <th></th>Price.
why.no-td: There is a header row but not enough data under it. Three rows by three columns — that is nine cells. An empty table with headings is a promise you did not keep.
hint2.no-td: Every value gets a cell of its own. Three things in a row — three <td>.
why.empty-td: There is an empty cell. An empty cell is not data — it is a hole. If something is missing, write it: a dash, a zero, none.
why.no-caption: The table has no name, or the name is empty. Lift it out of the page — numbers with no explanation. <caption> is the first thing inside <table>.
hint2.no-caption: The page heading does not do this job. It belongs to the page. The table wants a name of its own.
hint4.no-caption: Say what it holds and from when: Bike prices, July 2026.
why.no-h1: There is no page heading, or it is empty. One <h1>, with words in it.
why.no-page: The table is floating in mid-air. The task asks for a real page around it — a top part, navigation, main content, a bottom part. Data has to sit in something.
hint2.no-page: You know the skeleton from lesson 51. It repeats here — only now there is a table inside.
why.no-nav: There is no navigation with links. A menu without links is a list of words.
hint2.no-nav: Each item is an <a href="...">. The files do not exist yet and that is fine.
why.no-main: There is no main content. Say which part is the point of this page.
why.table-outside: The table is outside the main content. It IS the reason the page exists — its place is inside <main>.
hint2.table-outside: Check where you close the </main>. Close it before the table and the table stays outside.
why.no-footer: There is no bottom part, or it is empty. Put something in it — a copyright, a year, a contact.
blocks.2.text: Your topic again. This time the page must contain a COMPARISON — something that genuinely has rows and columns.
blocks.3.text: Whatever fits: prices of things, specs of models, a ranking, a timetable, results, a recipe breakdown. A header row and at least three rows of data under it, three columns, to be worth it. And a name for the table.
blocks.4.text: And around the table — a real page. A top part, navigation, main content, a bottom part. Everything you already know.
blocks.6.text: One <table> on the page. Exactly one. If you see a second — stop and ask yourself whether you are laying something out with it.
blocks.7.text: And the header row must be a header REALLY. Not a first row that looks different. A header.
blocks.9.text: Take a cell somewhere in the middle — not from the first row, not from the first column. Just a cell. Look at what is in it.
blocks.10.text: Now say out loud what it means. If you can — this is the price of the red bike — your table is real. If you cannot — this is… well, that is where the menu goes — you did not build a table. You built a grid.
blocks.11.text: Every cell has two addresses. If you cannot name them, it is not a table.
blocks.13.text: Start from the table, not the page. It is the core — everything else exists for it. Write the data first, then wrap it in what explains it.
blocks.14.text: And if your topic does not lend itself to a table — you have not looked at it hard enough. Everything has something to compare: prices, years, sizes, ratings, time.
blocks.16.text: Your table will not have a single line. No borders, no dividers. It will look like scattered words.
blocks.17.text: You know why by now — lines are CSS. But do not feel sorry too soon: the most beautiful tables you have ever seen are built from exactly these tags. Underneath it is the same <tr> and the same <td>.
